IRIS P, YANG
McDoNoucn, Horlawo & AnnN
A PRc)FESSIONAL CORPORATION
ATTORNEYS
(9r6) 444-39oo
TELECOPT Et: (9r6) 444-a334
sss cAprroL MALL, surrE eso 
r_.. ,-,,.r , ,.i .|:i t30
sACRAMENTo, cALTFoRNTA 9ser4 JIH t-'i 
:
(916t 
67 4-9796
TELEcoPTER 
(9r6) 
67r-o99o
BAY AREA 
OFF ICE
1999

STREET, 
SUITE 
]3OO
OAKLAND, 
CALIFORNIA 
94612
<4t5) 
444-73a?
]anuary 23,7990
Kathryn Donovan
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 I Street
Sacramento, CA 95874
Re: 
Conflict of Interest
Dear Ms. Donovan:
I am writing to you on behalf of the City of HaIf Moon Bay and one of
its city councilmembers regarding certain decisions to be made in the coming
months on a proposed redevelopment plan for the city. I am requesting
formal written advice pursuant to Section 83114(b) of the Government Code.
This letter supplements a previous letter sent to you by
Councilmember Iverson on ]anuary 79,7990, and the facts stated herein
should be considered in conjunction with the information provided in
Councilmember Iverson's letter.
The city has initiated proceedings to adopt a redevelopment plan for
the North Wavecrest Redevelopment Project. The project area for the
proposed Redevelopment PIan includes, in part, the entire downtown
commercial area and two small residential neighborhoods of the city. It is
anticipated that the plan may include public infrastructure improvements for
the residential areas in the Project Area. In addition, the plan may provide
for loans or other types of assistance to residential property owners and
tenants in these areas for rehabilitation or other types of private
improvements.
The city has a total population of approximately 9,000 persons and is
less than 10 square miles in size. The proposed project area represents about
10 or 15 percent of the existing residential area of the city.


Kathryn Donovan
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
January 23,1990
Page2
Councilmember David Iverson owns a home within one of the
residential areas included in the proposed redevelopment project area. As
the owner of an interest in real property within the project area, may
Councilman Iverson participate in decisions on the adoption of the proposed
redevelopment plan?
We would appreciate your response as quickly as possible. If you have
any questions, please feel free to call me.
IPY:pjp
cc: David Iverson
Mark Weiss
Robert Lanzone
Very truly yours,
9- /. 4^p
Iris P. Yang 
t 
/
McDoNoucH, HoLLAND & ALLEN
APROFESSIONALCORPOFAIION


460 Poplar Street
Half Moon Bay, California 9019
April 10, 190
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
sacramenro, cA 958&l-0807
RE: YOIIR FILE NT MBER A-90{67
Dear Members Of The Commissiorr"-
I un&rstand that itr correspordence dated February ?6, 190, Mr. Jorrcthan S. Rothman of pur trgal Division
provided written guidance to a Srramento law lirm regarding my eligbility to vote on redevelopment matters
in Half Moon Bay - guidance which *as based upon such frls as had been presented to him.
Unfortunately, not all facts were presented to Mr. RothmaL
This letter places many omitted facts before 1ou" and seek lour help, in the form of a Commission opinion, in
protecting my electoral constituency from a virulent strain of political disenfranchisement that was never intended
nor enisiorrcd by the electorate of California wlren it enacte4 by initiative, the Political Reform Act of 1974.
FACTS
There is always the danger, for development interestE that pointed questions put to lhose interests by an elected
ollicial -- if that omcial is allowed to speak - will result in important decisiors being made in that greatest of
a[ political disinfectants: tlre surxhine.
The recent result of Half Moon Ba/s dominance by development interests has been (1) unsightly high-density
development - as any visual inspection of Half Moon Bay would readily confrm, (2) proposed high-dersity
development of Half Moon Ba1/s coastal bluffs - der€lopment on such a scale as would inexorably overwhelm
lhe cit/s existing infrastructure, notwithstanding expeDsive "see nothing trear nothing speak nothing' 'studies"
and "reviews' that are about to b€ undertaken by hired "experts', and (3) brazen political strategies in furtherance
of high-density development.
Aggressive derrclopment presents extremely serious issues for tbe voters of any town. All too ofteq developers,
their adviserg and cooperative city ofiicials are nowhere to be found when it linally becomes apparent that
development has necrssitated further additions to school.q highwa3rs, etc..
Travel into Half Moon Bay on existing roads is already severely restricted by existing tralfic loads, with
substantial highway relief nowhere in sight. Other future infrashucture concerns abound, as vested interests
prfi€ed to push quick development schemes.
Al this vgt 1im. rrrlrcn informed city decisions require disinterested advice without even the appearanc€ of bias,
at least some of Half Moon Ba/s key redevelopment advisers are ultimately being funded by certain development
interests, und€r a reimbursement arrargement with the city.
At this very time when informed city decisions require disinterested and pointed questioning on the part of
elected city olficials, my home was rather interestingly irrcluded in a proposed 'redevelopment' 'study area", and
the FPPC was conlacte4 resultfug in preliminary guidance that I was ineligible to pafiicipate in redevelopment
and related growth issues. Intereslhgly, this removed liom those growth deliberations the or y Half Moon Bay
city council menber who was beginning to ask pointed questions.
Half Moon Bay citizens have not been receiving the fortkight arswers they deserve regarding the impact of the


FPPC
April 10, 1990
proposed high-density development on their taxes, trallig schoolg life sty'es, property \alues, etc..
For example, some city advisers early on suggested that the use of a redevelopment agency in Half Moon Bay
would harrc no adrrcrse tax impact on citizens living outside of such redevelopment area -- which turned out to
be "not entirely true', when the town citizenry began to i rcstigate this subjecr.
Agafust a backdrop of pressures for quick development, I ran for office atrd was elected on November 7, 1989
on a platform which wa.s unique among the other city coulrcil candidates, 1o put it mildly. My platform
emphasized balanced, sober, and sane growth, emphasizing the preservation of our regiorx's natural beauties,
wilhin an overall context of sound d€velopment.
Indeed, I am not against growlh per se. Wlat I am agaiNt is the further destruclion of those qualities which
made our coastal area what it once was. What I am in favor of is reasonable corr€ssions for our town's citizens
from any would-be developer.
In short, my candida.,'r, addressed some very disturbing problems, gi\,€n rec€nt happening on your and my
Califomia coast.
As 1ou may know, California's irrreasingly controversial Community Redevelopment hw enables a city to form
a redevelopment agency, condemn all property within a subs€quently determined redevelopment are4 and then
sell such condemned property lo favored development interests in a below-market, non-bid, sweetheart deal, with
local taxpayers outside of the rederrelopment area and competing cities being the losers.
The potential, in this process, for destruction of the integrity, or appearance of integity, of our local governments
can be seen from the magnitude of the subsidies that are possible under existing law. In the case of one very
small subsidy, the City of Pacfica's redevelopment agency recently sold to a developer for $60,000 a property
it had acquired for $176,000. In the case of much larger d€alE the potenlial fm abuse rises exponentially.
Developers often urge this 're'development agency technique upon a city as a low-cost - to them -- mearu of
elimirnting small landowners in lhe areas tlrey want to develop (- not redevelop). In these situations, the about
to be subsidized developers usually protest that their prolit margirrs will be tm low in th€ absence of subsidies.
Given that development project sizes in California are often well into the tens and even hundreds of millions of
dollars, the potential for money-fed destruction of our political proc€sses should be incredibly apparent to all
except the most naive - a situation rvhich tlre California l-eeislature should immediatelv nut on its remedial
apenda.
Under the Community Redevelopment Law, (l) redevelopment cannot proceed in the absenc€ of a legally
sustainable finding of "blight', and (2) areas of redevelopmeni within a city need not be contiguous.
These Iacts allow a sedous an4 in our opiniorL unlawful political abuse:
A development-oriented majority oI a tovn council, sitting as a rcdevelopment agency, can inclu& the home
of any 'serm growth' minority member in 1 'proposed' 'rcdevelopment' 'study area', even lhough his or her
n€ighborhood is not 'blighted' un&r the lew, 'stud/ the question of vhether therc is 'blight' in his or her
neighborhmd - potentially rtsulting under the Political Reform Act of 194, in his or her total exclusion from
any discussion of the re&velopment and rclated growth issues, until such time - much later in lhe process,
and after many crucial decisiorx have alrtady boen ma& - es it is lirnlly 'determined' that his or her
neighborhood wes not blighied alter all.
This is the substance of wtut has happered to me in Half Moon Ban and I am hertby appealing for both your
jurisdictionel end politiczl help in rcenfranchising a gnat number of voters of Half Moon Bay who arr
ext:mely worried about thc frrturt of their city.
We believe that in enacting by initiative the Polilical Reform Act of 1974, tlrc California electorate never
-2-


-3-
April 10, 190
intended that legislation to apply to a situation such as mine, r,here the effect would be the complete eradication
of potentially embarrassing questions put to development interesls, and lhe complete political gutting of a 'sane
growth' corstituency, amidst self-serving conteotions by a derelopment-oriented city council majority that city
council nembers are elected by an entire city, ard not, in any resp€ct, by mncerned citizen constituencies.
In the hstant case, I lirae in a neighborhood called Adeta Park. It is imoortant to note lhat Arleta Park and
its surroundinps er: no more bliqhted then thc rcsoective neishborhoods of the other city council members
of Half Moon Bav (-- m4 agafuL without a legally sustahable frndhg of'blight', the use of the redevelopment
agency device is illegal).
In fact, Arleta Park is a thriving residential neighborhood with new construction and high property walues which
have appreciated by an annual arrerage in excess of 2096 over the past three years.
In fact, home prices in Arleta Park presently range from approximately $250,000 to approximately 925,000.
California statutes and case la\v characterize "blighted' areas as thGe with a higher street cdme rate, a higher
arson rale, a higher percentage of ursafe structures, a higher percentage of dilapidated structures, etc..
Under California law, irregular parcelization cannot, in and of itse[ support a hnding of blight, nor can open
spaces be considered blighted. Thus, the area near Arleta Park is not eligible for redevelopment either.
Nonetheless, other city ofEcids recently (1) included my mighborhood Arleta Parlq in a proposed
re&velopment 'stu$/ are, along vith lands held by major &velopment interrsts, (2) arranged for the FPPC
to be contacted, and (3) angrily rrfused to hold tlre rc&velopment process in abeyance until the FPPC formally
opined on ny elegibility to parlicipate in r:&velopment matters.
I ask the FPPC to ertfully note that at a rcctnt rcd€velopment m€cdn& tlle city admitted that no written legal
opinion of counsel exists which provi&s that my neighborhood, Arleta Parlq is 'blighted', under California lav.
Indeed, uc believe llrere is no such written legal opinion of counsel, because &scribing Arleta Park as
'blighted" would likely erposc such counsel to charges of malprecticc or worsc at a later stage. We further
believe that in thc manifest absence of 'blight', alloling e rc&velopment agency mechanism to procceq
consuming public r:sources, could also constitutc malprecticc-
In short, neilher Arelta Park, nor any other area presently in the redevelopment "study area" is blighted as blight
is defined under California statute and case law, despite absurd arguments to the contrary, regarding irregular
parcelizatioq etc., in the face of clear court rulings.
So far, regarding Arleta Park, the city murrcil majority and c€rtah advisers have hung their hats on an assertion
that my neighborhood might benefit from improved sewers or sidewaiks - an ass€rtion that applies equally weli
to most of HaIf Moon Bay (- sewer improvement even befug an annourred city-wide goal). It has even been
suggested that if Arleta Park residents do not go along with their inclusion in the redevelopment area, they could
face assessments from a tlueatened assessment district for improvements to sewers, etc., even though Arleta Park
citizers are hardly crying out for such 'improvements'.
Further, even if my neighborhood could benefit from fancier sewers, the economic life of its present sewers is
not at an end and, given the recent Arleta Park appreciation, th€ present sewers have obviously not ended my
thrivirg neighborhood's overall economic use.
Please note that the city has not formally responded in detail to questions of the many citizens who have asked
how Arleta Park could possibly be rblighted'. Instead the cit/s current stratery is to say that 'engineers' will
be sent in to "studly' Arleta Park's sewerg etc., and then legal coursel will opine as to whether the 'engineers"
'nndings' mean tlEt Arleta Park is "blighted".
REOUEST


FPPC
April 10, 1990
It is time for such unfair political maneurrcrirgs to be seen for wlut they are.
Acordingly, I ask the Commissioa
(1) 
To declare that the Political Reform Act of 1974 was never intended to aoolv to facts such as these.
esoecially where no formal written leeal opinion of counsel exists claiminq - and riskine the maloractic.. etc.
of claiminq - lhat my miphborhood is 'blighted - and that. therefore. I mav naflicioate in all redevelooment
and relaled srowth issues.
I respectfully submit that every interpreter of the literal wording of our statutes, and the rules promulgated
thereunder, should cotrtinually ask the litmus test question ofwhether those statutes and rules were ever intended
to apply to pa icular fact situations.
It seems to me that tle abserre ofe formal vritten leqal oninion ol couns€l that mv neiehborhood is'bliphted'.
as &fined bv California law. is a crucial ooint uoon rihich the FPFC can articulate a distinction in interpretinp
even existinp law.
<2) 
To recommend, if (1) is not seen presently possible, alternatives short of lhe utter and mmplete
disenfranchisement of my Half Moon Bay constituency, in line with existing FPPC pronouncements supporting
alternatives short of tlre complete disenfranchisement of voter constituencies.
(3) 
For its jurisdictional ard political support in ending a[ political maneuverings cloaked as conllict of
interest issues, such as has occurred in my case.
(4) 
To use any iflrcstigative powers it may harre with respect to recent developments in Half Moon Bay.
On behalf of thousands of extremely concerned voters in Half Moon Bay, I especially ask for )our jurisdictional
and political help in ensuring that the pointed questions do get asked of development interests.
If they are not so asked, upon your next drive to the disappearing beauty of California's coast, you will be greeted
by yet more abhorrent memorials to developer greed.
Many persons besides myself are seeking to preserve important coastal values, views, and life styles, while at the
same time remaining sensitiw to tlre concerns of otherq including developers.
Development is not, per se, unacceptable -- but ugly, high-density, "hil and run' coastal development is.
I an among the many California citizens who applaud your elTorts on behalf of fair practices in politics. Half
Moon Bay aod I now ask for your jurisdictional and political help.
Sincerely,
)--:r3.-*.--
Senator Marian Bergeson
Senator Quentin Kopp
Assemblyman Ted lrmFrt
Senator Rebecca Morgan
Senate Committee On Local Gorrernment
-4-
David Iverson

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city councilmember who owns residential property within a proposed redevelopment project area may participate in decisions regarding adoption of the redevelopment plan, and whether the FPPC's preliminary guidance excluding him was legally and procedurally appropriate.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (January 23) and follow-up correspondence/appeal (April 10) challenging FPPC's preliminary guidance. Neither section constitutes an FPPC response or advice letter itself. The April 10 letter is partially truncated. Document ID 90067 matches the reference in the April 10 letter ('YOUR FILE NUMBER A-90-67'). High confidence on document type and structure; moderate confidence on synthesizing the councilmember's complex arguments in his April 10 letter.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether a city councilmember who owns residential property within a proposed redevelopment project area may participate in decisions regarding adoption of the redevelopment plan, and whether the FPPC's preliminary guidance excluding him was legally and procedurally appropriate.
embedding.first_500_words: IRIS P, YANG McDoNoucn, Horlawo & AnnN A PRc)FESSIONAL CORPORATION ATTORNEYS (9r6) 444-39oo TELECOPT Et: (9r6) 444-a334 sss cAprroL MALL, surrE eso r_.. ,-,,.r , ,.i .|:i t30 sACRAMENTo, cALTFoRNTA 9ser4 JIH t-'i : (916t 67 4-9796 TELEcoPTER (9r6) 67r-o99o BAY AREA OFF ICE 1999 HARRISON STREET, SUITE ]3OO OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612 <4t5) 444-73a? ]anuary 23,7990 Kathryn Donovan General Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission 428 I Street Sacramento, CA 95874 Re: Conflict of Interest Dear Ms. Donovan: I am writing to you on behalf of the City of HaIf Moon Bay and one of its city councilmembers regarding certain decisions to be made in the coming months on a proposed redevelopment plan for the city. I am requesting formal written advice pursuant to Section 83114(b) of the Government Code. This letter supplements a previous letter sent to you by Councilmember Iverson on ]anuary 79,7990, and the facts stated herein should be considered in conjunction with the information provided in Councilmember Iverson's letter. The city has initiated proceedings to adopt a redevelopment plan for the North Wavecrest Redevelopment Project. The project area for the proposed Redevelopment PIan includes, in part, the entire downtown commercial area and two small residential neighborhoods of the city. It is anticipated that the plan may include public infrastructure improvements for the residential areas in the Project Area. In addition, the plan may provide for loans or other types of assistance to residential property owners and tenants in these areas for rehabilitation or other types of private improvements. The city has a total population of approximately 9,000 persons and is less than 10 square miles in size. The proposed project area represents about 10 or 15 percent of the existing residential area of the city. Kathryn Donovan General Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission January 23,1990 Page2 Councilmember David Iverson owns a home within one of the residential areas included in the proposed redevelopment project area. As the owner of an interest in real property within the project area, may Councilman Iverson participate in decisions on the adoption of the proposed redevelopment plan? We would appreciate your response as quickly as possible. If you have any questions, please feel free to call me. embedding.summary: This is a two-part document: an initial request letter (January 23, 1990) from attorney Iris P. Yang seeking FPPC advice on conflict of interest rules for a councilmember with property in a proposed redevelopment area, and a follow-up letter (April 10, 1990) from the councilmember himself challenging preliminary FPPC guidance and arguing his exclusion from redevelopment votes represents political disenfranchisement motivated by development interests seeking to silence a growth-skeptical council voice.
